definition: The average surprisal of a source's symbols, equal to the minimum average number of bits per symbol needed to encode it losslessly.
The information content of a single outcome x, equal to -log2 p(x) bits; rare events carry more bits.
Shannon's result that average lossless code length cannot fall below the entropy H(X) but can approach it arbitrarily closely.
The gap between how many bits a representation actually uses and the entropy floor; what compression removes.
Encoding that allows bit-for-bit exact reconstruction of the original input.
Encoding that discards information to return an approximation, governed by rate-distortion theory rather than entropy.
Shannon's theory giving the minimum bit rate R(D) needed to represent a source within a tolerated distortion D.
The extra bits per symbol incurred by coding with a wrong model q instead of the true distribution p.
The length of the shortest program that outputs a specific string x; the absolute, uncomputable notion of its compressed size.
The 1960s framework (Solomonoff, Kolmogorov, Chaitin) defining information content of individual objects via shortest-program length.
Kolmogorov complexity depends on the choice of universal machine only up to an additive constant, making it well-defined asymptotically.
An entropy coder that turns next-symbol probabilities directly into a near-optimal bitstream of length about -log2 q(x).
The exact equivalence by which any next-symbol predictor yields a compressor and any compressor implicitly defines a predictor.
The component that converts a probability model's estimate P(symbol) into bits, aiming to spend about -log2 P(symbol) bits per symbol.
The standard architecture separating the probability model (which predicts P over symbols) from the coder (which writes bits), so each can be improved independently.
A code in which no codeword is a prefix of another, letting a decoder parse symbols unambiguously left-to-right.
A 1952 greedy tree-building algorithm producing the optimal prefix code, limited by forcing a whole number of bits per symbol.
The inability of any prefix code to spend fractional bits, causing overpayment when symbol probabilities are not powers of 1/2.
A method that encodes an entire message as a single subinterval of [0,1), reaching the entropy limit with no per-symbol rounding.
Rescaling the current interval (or state) and emitting settled bits as coding proceeds, allowing finite-precision operation in bounded memory.
An arithmetic-coding variant operating on an integer range in an arbitrary base (often byte-wise), historically valued for sidestepping IBM's patents.
IBM's binary arithmetic coder (Q-coder) refined with Mitsubishi, patented and specified in JPEG/JBIG, whose licensing uncertainty deterred adoption.
Duda's 2009 entropy coder that encodes a message into a single growing integer state, achieving arithmetic-coding ratios at Huffman-like speed.
The table-based (finite-state-machine) and range variants of ANS; tANS needs no per-symbol multiply, rANS handles adaptive probabilities.
Compression by replacing repeated data with short references back to an earlier occurrence, rather than re-emitting the data itself.
Lempel & Ziv's 1977 scheme that encodes data as (distance, length, literal) back-references into a window of recently seen bytes.
Lempel & Ziv's 1978 scheme that builds an explicit numbered dictionary of phrases and emits (index, next-symbol) pairs.
A (distance, length) pointer telling the decoder to copy a run of bytes it has already reconstructed.
Welch's 1984 LZ78 variant emitting only dictionary indices; used in GIF and Unix compress, and at the center of the Unisys patent dispute.
Phil Katz's LZ77 + Huffman combination (RFC 1951, 1996) that underlies zlib, gzip, PNG, ZIP, and HTTP gzip encoding.
An arithmetic-style entropy coder using context models, used by LZMA to beat Huffman's integer-bit overhead.
A reversible permutation (used by bzip2) that clusters similar contexts together so a following entropy coder compresses better.
Finite State Entropy, Yann Collet's implementation of Asymmetric Numeral Systems (Duda, 2009), giving arithmetic-coding optimality at table-lookup speed; the entropy stage of zstd.
A custom dictionary built from sample records so a codec like zstd can effectively compress many small, similar payloads.
A statistical compressor that predicts the next symbol from the longest matching recent context, emitting an escape symbol to back off to shorter contexts when a symbol is unseen.
The question of how much probability to reserve for never-yet-seen symbols in a context; PPM variants A/B/C/D differ chiefly in how they answer it.
Running many predictive models in parallel and combining all their probability estimates into one prediction per bit, rather than choosing a single context as PPM does.
Combining models in the log-odds (stretch) domain with a weighted sum and a logistic squash, equivalent to a single-layer neural network trained online to minimize coding cost.
A learned calibration table that refines a mixed probability to correct the mixer's systematic over- or under-confidence.
Matt Mahoney's open context-mixing compressor family (PAQ1, 2002 onward) that pioneered neural/logistic mixing and dominated text-compression ratio benchmarks.
Mahoney's archival format (2009-2015) that embeds the decompression model as bytecode in the archive, so one fixed program can decode files from future, smarter compressors.
Byron Knoll's context-mixing compressor (begun ~2014) with a very large model ensemble including an LSTM; basis of the current Hutter Prize record.
The first 100 MB and 1 GB of an English Wikipedia dump, used as the standard text-compression benchmark files.
Marcus Hutter's contest (2006; 500k euro from 2020) paying for record-setting lossless compression of enwik9 under tight single-core, no-GPU, 10 GB RAM limits.
The length of the shortest program that outputs a given string; the uncomputable theoretical limit of compression.

Solomonoff's optimal universal predictor weights hypotheses by 2^(-program length); AIXI is Hutter's formal model of optimal agency built on it, grounding the claim that compression equals prediction equals intelligence.
A bijective rearrangement of data that preserves information content but reshapes the symbol stream so a downstream entropy coder compresses it better.
Replacing a maximal run of one repeated symbol with a (symbol, count) pair, cheap to compute and most effective as the final stage after a transform has manufactured long runs.
A reversible permutation of a block obtained by sorting all its cyclic rotations and emitting the last column, which clusters together characters that share a common following context.
A sorted array of the starting positions of all suffixes of a string; computing it (in O(n) time/space) is how the BWT is built efficiently instead of forming the full rotation matrix.
The property that the i-th occurrence of a symbol in the BWT's last column matches the i-th occurrence in the first column, enabling linear-time inversion and FM-index search.
A transform keeping a symbol list and outputting each symbol's current position then moving it to the front, mapping local repetition into small integers and long runs of zeros.
A self-index (Ferragina & Manzini, 2000) storing the BWT plus small tables that counts and locates pattern occurrences in time proportional to pattern length, on near-compressed-size space.
A BWT-based full-text index using O(r) space where r is the number of BWT runs, designed for highly repetitive collections like pangenomes.

Shannon's information-theoretic minimum bit-rate needed to represent a source within average distortion D, equal to the minimum mutual information I(X;X̂) over reconstructions meeting the distortion constraint.
A function d(x,x̂) quantifying how bad it is to reproduce source value x as x̂; its choice (squared error vs. perceptual) determines what the codec optimizes.
The three-stage skeleton of nearly all lossy codecs: change coordinates, round coefficients (the lossy step), then losslessly pack the result.
Properties of a good transform: concentrating signal energy into a few coefficients and making coefficients statistically independent so each can be quantized cheaply and independently.
The optimal but signal-dependent transform (eigenbasis of the covariance matrix / PCA) that perfectly decorrelates a stationary Gaussian source.
A fixed, fast, signal-independent transform (Ahmed-Natarajan-Rao, 1974) that closely approximates the KLT for correlated natural signals and underlies JPEG and video codecs.
Mapping a fine-grained value to one of a finite set of levels (e.g., divide by a step and round); the only irreversible, lossy step in the pipeline.
Designing the codec to minimize distortion humans actually perceive, exploiting models of vision and hearing rather than mathematical error.
The auditory phenomenon where a loud sound renders nearby quieter sounds inaudible; a psychoacoustic model computes the masking threshold below which quantization noise is imperceptible.
Johnston's measure of the number of bits of perceptually relevant information in an audio signal, accounting for masking.
A critically-sampled transform using 50%-overlapping windows whose time-domain aliasing cancels exactly (TDAC), giving artifact-free block transitions; used in MP3, AAC, AC-3, Opus.
The principle (Princen-Bradley, 1986) by which deliberately introduced aliasing in overlapping MDCT blocks cancels on reconstruction, enabling overlap without redundancy.
Learned compression replacing the fixed transform with a trained autoencoder optimized jointly for rate + λ·distortion, a gradient-descent relaxation of R(D).
Blau-Michaeli's result that low distortion and high perceptual realism are distinct conflicting goals, so allowing imperceptible distortion enables better-looking low-rate reconstructions.
A linear transform expressing an 8×8 pixel block as a weighted sum of fixed cosine patterns, concentrating most energy into a few low-frequency coefficients so the rest can be discarded.
Dividing transform coefficients by a quantization-table value and rounding to integers; the irreversible step that discards perceptually unimportant (mostly high-frequency) information.
The lossless final stage (Huffman, arithmetic, or ANS) that packs the quantized integers into the fewest bits given their statistics.
Visible tile edges at low JPEG quality caused by quantizing each 8×8 block independently so neighbors no longer match at shared borders.
Gibbs-like oscillations near sharp edges, because reconstructing a discontinuity needs high-frequency coefficients that quantization has removed.
Storing color (chroma) at lower spatial resolution than brightness (luma), exploiting the eye's weaker color acuity to discard ~75% of color data.
A whole-image transform into nested resolution bands localized in space and frequency, used by JPEG 2000 to avoid blocking and enable progressive decoding.
The difference between a pixel and its prediction from neighbors; storing small residuals instead of raw values (as PNG does) decorrelates smooth regions.
The LZ77-plus-Huffman lossless algorithm shared by zip, gzip, and PNG.
An entropy coder (Jarek Duda, 2007) matching arithmetic-coding ratios at near-Huffman decode speed, used by JPEG XL and Zstandard.
JPEG XL's ability to re-encode an existing JPEG ~20% smaller and reverse it bit-exactly, since both keep DCT coefficients and differ only in the entropy stage.
An image format built from a single still frame of a video codec (WebP from VP8, HEIC from HEVC, AVIF from AV1).
An encoder component that computes, per frequency band, the masking threshold below which quantization noise is inaudible, so bits can be allocated to keep error just under perceptibility.
The frequency-dependent sound level below which a tone cannot be heard at all; lowest around 2-4 kHz, so sub-threshold spectral energy can be discarded.
A loud tone raises the audibility threshold of nearby frequencies, hiding faint components within the same critical band so they can be coarsely quantized or dropped.
A loud transient hides quiet sounds for a few ms before and tens-to-hundreds of ms after it, allowing coarse quantization around loud onsets.
Frequency groups within which the cochlea integrates energy; masking and bit allocation are organized along this perceptual scale rather than uniform Hz.

A lapped, critically-sampled transform with 50% block overlap and time-domain aliasing cancellation, giving perfect reconstruction while suppressing block-boundary artifacts; the core transform of MP3, AAC, Vorbis and Opus.
The difference between an actual sample and a predictor's estimate of it; small residuals are cheap to entropy-code, which is the basis of lossless prediction-based coders like FLAC.
A parameterized entropy code that is near-optimal for geometric/Laplacian-distributed residuals, needing only one tunable parameter per partition; used by FLAC.
Estimating each sample as a weighted combination of previous samples, leaving a small residual to encode; underpins FLAC and the SILK layer of Opus.
Opus fuses Skype's linear-predictive speech coder (SILK) with Xiph's low-delay MDCT music coder (CELT), switching among speech-only, music-only and hybrid modes.
An Opus 1.5 neural feature embedding ~1 second of recovery data in packet padding to reconstruct audio through prolonged burst packet loss.
Predicting a block in the current frame by copying a displaced block from a previously decoded reference frame, encoding only a motion vector plus the residual.
A small (Δx, Δy) offset telling the decoder where in the reference frame to copy the prediction block from.
The difference between the true block and its motion-compensated (or intra) prediction, which is then transformed, quantized, and entropy-coded.
Intra frames coded alone, P-frames predicted from past frames, and B-frames predicted bidirectionally from both past and future references (the cheapest).
A repeating I-P-B... frame pattern; longer GOPs compress better but hurt seeking and error recovery.
The encoder choosing each coding option to minimize J = D + λ·R, trading reconstruction distortion D against bitrate R.
Standards define only the bitstream and decoder, so encoders vary widely in quality and are far more expensive than decoders (especially due to motion search).
A consortium licensing a bundle of essential patents for a codec; fragmented, overlapping pools and unaffiliated holders crippled HEVC/VVC adoption.
The Alliance for Open Media (formed 2015), an industry consortium producing royalty-free codecs AV1 and AV2.
Fixed-function decoder silicon in devices; without it, software decode drains battery, which is the structural deployment lag for AV1/AV2.
Using model Q on a source P costs H(P,Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P||Q) bits per symbol; the wasted bits above the entropy floor equal the KL divergence, i.e. the modeling error itself.
The training objective -log Q(x) for the next symbol, which is literally the number of bits needed to encode that symbol with the model, fusing learning and compression into one objective.
Building a single adaptive code that performs nearly as well as the best code in a class for any source in that class, paying only a vanishing per-symbol redundancy that shrinks with message length.
The excess bits a universal/adaptive coder spends over the ideal code for the true source; it provably decays as more data is seen.
A smoothed-count (add-1/2) probability estimator for unknown discrete sources whose universal cost is about (1/2)log2(n) extra bits per parameter — the price of learning one parameter from data.
Rissanen's 1978 principle: pick the model minimizing L(model)+L(data|model), the total bits to transmit hypothesis plus residual — a quantitative, self-regularizing Occam's razor.
An encoding that first transmits the model and then the data given the model; the simplest concrete instance of MDL, recovering the (1/2)log n per-parameter complexity penalty.
Solomonoff's universal prior (1960-64) assigning a string probability summed over all programs that output it, each weighted 2^(-program length), so short programs dominate.
The length of the shortest program that outputs x on a universal Turing machine — the ultimate model-free compressed size of an object; provably uncomputable.

The theoretically optimal universal predictor that Bayes-mixes over all programs; its cumulative error is bounded by the data source's Kolmogorov complexity, but it is uncomputable and serves as an ideal limit.
A codec where the transform, quantization surrogate, and entropy model are all neural networks trained jointly by gradient descent on a rate-distortion loss, rather than hand-designed.
The training objective R + λD, where R is the expected codelength under a learned probability model and D is distortion (often MSE); λ slides the codec along the rate-distortion curve.
A training trick that replaces non-differentiable rounding with adding uniform noise to the latent, providing a smooth surrogate so backpropagation can flow through the quantization step.
A small second autoencoder that transmits side information describing the local statistics (e.g. Gaussian scale) of the main latents, letting the entropy model adapt spatially across the image.
An entropy model that predicts each latent element from already-decoded neighbors, improving compression but forcing slow serial decoding.
A codec trained with a GAN or diffusion loss to make reconstructions match the distribution of natural images (realism), hallucinating plausible texture instead of minimizing pixel error.
Blau and Michaeli's 2019 result that perceptual realism is a third axis in tension with low distortion and low rate, explaining why MSE codecs look blurry at low bitrate.
Storing a signal as the weights of a small network overfit to it (e.g. an MLP mapping pixel coordinates to colors), as in COIN; decoding evaluates the network at each coordinate.
The DCVC approach to neural video that uses temporal context as a condition on the entropy model rather than computing an explicit predicted-frame residual.

An entropy coder that encodes each symbol in essentially -log2 p bits given a probability p; pairing it with an LLM's next-token distribution turns the model's prediction error into compressed output.
An LLM's cross-entropy training objective, Σ -log p(x_t|context), equals (up to log 2) the arithmetic-coded file length, making minimizing training loss and minimizing file size the same operation.
The -log p cost an arithmetic coder pays per token; near zero when the model confidently predicts the true token, large when the model is wrong.
Fabrice Bellard's online neural compressor that trains a Transformer on the file as it compresses, so no model needs to be shipped with the data.
Bellard's pretrained LLM text compressor using a frozen, 8-bit-quantized RWKV-169M model evaluated deterministically so the file can be decompressed on different hardware.
The honest measure of an LLM compressor's cost: compressed file bits plus the bits needed to store the model itself, which for huge models can dwarf the payload.
The empirical finding that a model's bits-per-character on held-out text correlates near-linearly (r about -0.95) with its benchmark capability scores.
Storing only the differences (position + mismatches) between data and a shared reference, rather than the data itself; the basis of the CRAM genomic format.
Per-base confidence estimates attached to sequencing reads; noisy, dominate genomic file size, and are usually compressed lossily.
Lossy compression that guarantees every reconstructed value lies within a user-set absolute or relative tolerance; used for HPC floating-point arrays.
Lindstrom's 2014 block-transform floating-point compressor that decorrelates 4^d blocks and bit-plane-codes coefficients, supporting random access into compressed arrays.
Di and Cappello's 2016 prediction-based error-bounded compressor that predicts each value from neighbors, quantizes the residual, and entropy-codes.
Replacing repeated values (e.g. strings) with small integer IDs from a per-column dictionary; a core Parquet/ORC technique.
Encoding consecutive repeated values as a single value plus a repeat count.
Storing integers using only as many bits as their range requires instead of a full machine word.
Encoding the difference between successive differences; makes regularly-spaced timestamps nearly free, central to Gorilla time-series compression.
Hinton et al. (2015) technique training a small student model to mimic a large teacher's soft output distribution, transferring information beyond hard labels.
Removing weights with small absolute value to produce a sparse, lower-description-length network (Han et al., Deep Compression, 2015).
A 2022 one-shot post-training weight quantization method that quantizes column-by-column using Hessian information to compensate each rounding error in remaining weights.
A 2024 method that protects salient weights identified by activation magnitude via rescaling, enabling accurate 4-bit LLM quantization.
The stored key/value vectors for all past tokens in an LLM; grows with context length and dominates inference memory, making it a prime compression target.
DeepSeek-V2/V3 attention variant that jointly projects keys and values into a small cached latent, cutting KV memory ~93% while matching full-attention quality.
Jegou et al. (2011) vector-compression method splitting a vector into sub-vectors, each replaced by the nearest of a small set of learned centroids, enabling fast table-lookup distance search.
Compressing embeddings to 1 bit per dimension with Hamming-distance search, giving ~32x compression at ~80-95% retrieval recall.
